RESOLVED (previously a dead end): the regiment is the 150th NY Volunteer Infantry, the actual 'Dutchess County Regiment,' recruited almost entirely from Dutchess County and organized at Poughkeepsie. Confirmed via multiple independent sources (NY State Military Museum, Wikipedia, FamilySearch) that it left the State October 11, 1862 -- one day before this ledger entry, consistent with a Philadelphia transit the following morning. The regiment's real commander was Col. John H. Ketcham, not 'Randall' as the ledger records. Leading theory: Col. Francis V. Randall's 13th Vermont Infantry ALSO left its home state on October 11, 1862, and arrived in Washington D.C. on October 13 (confirmed via Wikipedia and other sources) -- the exact same departure date as the 150th NY. Since the standard rail corridor from both New York and New England to Washington ran through Philadelphia, both regiments were very likely passing through the city around the same time (Oct 12), giving a concrete, date-verified mechanism for the ledger-keeper to have mixed up the two regiments' officers. Not independently confirmed: no primary source explicitly names a Philadelphia or Cooper Shop stop for either regiment specifically -- this ledger entry itself is the best direct evidence of that stop, for whichever regiment it actually records. A secondary, weaker possibility remains later conflation with Col. Charles B. Randall of the 149th NY (Onondaga County), who fought alongside the 150th NY at Gettysburg -- though that connection formed after this Oct 1862 date and doesn't explain a contemporaneous recording error as well as the 13th VT coincidence does.
